g.data-narration: Welcome to Propositional Logic, the foundation of mathematical reasoning and computer science.
A proposition is a declarative statement that is either definitely true or false, but not both.
g.text: A declarative statement that is either TRUE or FALSE, but not both

g.data-narration: Atomic propositions are simple statements like p, q, r. Compound propositions combine them using logical connectives.
g.text: p, q, r, s - Cannot be broken down further
p ∧ q, ¬p ∨ r - Combined using connectives

g.data-narration: Propositional logic uses five connectives: negation, conjunction, disjunction, implication, and biconditional.

g.text: IFF, "p if and only if q"

g.data-narration: Truth tables show all possible truth value combinations. Negation flips the truth value. Conjunction needs both true.
Implication p→q is FALSE only when p is TRUE and q is FALSE. It's vacuously true when antecedent is false.
g.text: Implication (p → q) - THE KEY TO UNDERSTANDING
FALSE only when p=TRUE and q=FALSE. True otherwise (vacuously true).

g.data-narration: Biconditional p↔q is true when both have the same truth value. It's equivalent to (p→q) ∧ (q→p).
g.text: TRUE when p and q have SAME truth value. FALSE when different.

g.data-narration: For n propositions, truth tables have 2^n rows. Let's construct one for (p ∧ q) → (p ∨ q).
g.text: Example: Truth Table for (p ∧ q) → (p ∨ q)
For 2 propositions: 2² = 4 rows. Let's evaluate each combination.

g.data-narration: Two propositions are logically equivalent if they have identical truth values for all assignments, shown by ≡ symbol.
g.text: Two propositions P and Q are equivalent if they have identical truth values for ALL truth assignments.

g.data-narration: Tautology is always true, contradiction always false, contingency depends on truth values of components.
Important logical laws include identity, domination, idempotent, commutative, associative, and distributive laws.
De Morgan's laws show how negation distributes over conjunction and disjunction, flipping the operator.
CNF is conjunction of disjunctions (AND of ORs). DNF is disjunction of conjunctions (OR of ANDs).
An argument is valid if whenever all premises are true, the conclusion is also true. Check by finding counterexamples.
